summary: DHH argues that open source software development should not be framed in market-based, transactional terms. Drawing on anthropological history of debt, Maslow's hierarchy of needs, and existentialist philosophy, he contends that the 'tragedy of the commons' is a conceptual misappropriation for software because software has zero marginal cost — freeloaders are literally free. He rejects both the GPL's forced-reciprocity model and proprietary licensing as rooted in the same scarcity-based worldview, championing instead the MIT license as a vehicle for genuine altruism. He frames his two decades of unpaid Rails work not as 'free labor' but as a source of profound meaning, autonomy, and self-transcendence, and declares a 'jubilee' releasing all imagined debts to the Rails community.
key_points: The 'tragedy of the commons' doesn't apply to open source software because software has zero marginal cost — more users don't deplete the resource
Both Bill Gates and Richard Stallman built their worldviews on scarcity-based thinking and strong property rights, just enforced in opposite directions
The MIT license is fundamentally different from copyleft licenses — it's an anti-license that embraces genuine giving without reciprocal obligations
Open source burnout often stems not from overwork but from loss of meaning — when contributors start framing their work in market terms, the joy dies
Meaning in work is recursive and self-sustaining: meaningful work creates a meaningful life which inspires more meaningful work
money_quotes: The magic of software is that there is virtually no marginal cost! The freeloaders are free! There is no practical scarcity to worry about.

When I look at the literally billions of dollars in business that's been done on the basis of this thing I started, I don't look at that with envy or an open mouth. I don't think 'I should have had some of that'. I think what a wonderful world!
Neither Shopify nor Twitter, nor any other company or person, holds the power to cause tragedy to our commons in Rails. There is no tragedy, there will be no tragedy.
Gates was afraid that users would take his software and not pay him for it. Stallman was afraid that users would extend his software and not hand over their contributions.
If you dress up like a salesman, it's a little disingenuous to be surprised when people think they're buying a product.
For fuck's sake, Dropbox. You host files. You make the files appear on all my computers.

Open source, as seen through the altruistic lens of the MIT gift license, has the power to break us free from this overly rational cost-benefit analysis bullshit that's impoverishing our lives in so many other ways.
Contribute to the Rails community because it brings meaning to your life. Because writing Ruby sparks joy. Don't participate if it doesn't.
key_insight: Open source software has zero marginal cost, making the entire scarcity-based framing of 'sustainability,' 'free labor,' and 'tragedy of the commons' a conceptual error that poisons what should be a source of meaning and self-transcendence.
